title: Interview Opening in Albany, Georgia
summary: Interviewer Joe Mosnier introduces the June 4, 2011 session for the Civil Rights History Project, recorded at Albany State University during the 53rd Reunion of the Southwest Georgia Civil Rights Movement, and welcomes Charles McDew.
related_events: 53rd Reunion of the Southwest Georgia Civil Rights Movement

title: Born in Massillon, a Steel and Football Town
summary: McDew recounts his birth on June 23, 1938 in Massillon, Ohio, a community of about 38,000 known for steel and football. His father and grandfather played for Paul Brown's Massillon Tigers, the Pro Football Hall of Fame in Canton sits eight miles away, and his father worked for Republic Steel.

title: Union Heritage in the Steel Mills
summary: McDew describes growing up active in United Steel Workers Local 1124, where his grandfather, uncles, and father all worked the mills. The teams he played for, the parks, and the fields were all produced by the unions.

title: Integrated Neighborhood Schools Up North
summary: McDew recalls Massillon's good, highly integrated neighborhood schools, where he went twelve years with the same children. His sister was one of the first Black teachers in Massillon public schools, and his father trained as a teacher but could not be hired in the North before the 1954 Brown decision. Of 38,000 residents, roughly 3,500 were Black.
related_events: 1954 Brown v. Board of Education decision

title: Supporting Bobby Grier at the Bowl Game
topic: Bobby Grier and the Bowl Game
summary: McDew describes early civil rights awareness from football: traveling as a child from Massillon to Pittsburgh, about 120 miles, to support Bobby Grier of the University of Pittsburgh after bowl organizers refused to play a team with a Black player. His parents said Grier was one of us and they had to help.
related_events: Bowl game segregation controversy over Bobby Grier

title: Writing Letters to James Lawson
summary: McDew recalls writing letters of support to James Lawson, an advisor to Dr. King and minister at his mother's St. James A.M.E. Church, while Lawson was jailed as a conscientious objector. He also names his uncle Horace Gillom, an end and punter for the Cleveland Browns, and reflects on how football carried civil rights issues into Massillon.

title: Massillon as a Town of Worker Immigrants
summary: McDew frames Massillon as an immigrant culture and an expression of the American dream, with people drawn from Greece, Spain, Yugoslavia, Czechoslovakia, and the American South. He argues Southerners were as much immigrants as the Czechs, coming to enjoy the fruits of citizenship and decent jobs, schools, and health care for the first time.

title: Coxey's Army and First Black Nurses
summary: McDew describes learning history from old men at the barbershop, including General Jacob Coxey himself and grandfathers who had marched in Coxey's Army of the poor. He recounts how his aunts and mother became some of the first Black nurses, his mother studying at Homer G. Phillips in St. Louis before nursing work opened in Massillon.

title: Learning Supply and Demand From Steel
summary: McDew explains how he learned economics from the rhythms of Mesabi iron ore arriving in Massillon, becoming steel, going to Chicago and Detroit, then cycling back. Full employment and overtime produced stockpiles, followed by layoffs, teaching him supply and demand and the swing between times of plenty and scarcity when families gathered coal off trains.

title: A Football Player's Plan for Life
summary: McDew recalls that Massillon produced steel and football players, and he was a football player. By ninth grade he had his life mapped out: play ball at Michigan, turn pro, marry, have smart kids, make money, and retire to a business like other players did.

title: His Father's Plan to Send Him South
summary: McDew describes his father's insistence that each child attend a historically Black college for at least one year, sending him to South Carolina State College in Orangeburg, where his father had graduated. The aim was to expose him to a professional class of Black people beyond the athletes and preachers who were the only visible role models up North.
related_events: McDew's matriculation at South Carolina State, fall 1959

title: The Drive South and First Segregation
summary: McDew recounts driving Route 21 from Massillon to Orangeburg in fall 1959, having never been South. His father warned him about segregated bathrooms and food once they crossed into West Virginia, his mother packed lunches because no places would feed Black travelers, and the world grew stranger the further they went.

title: Paradise at South Carolina State
summary: McDew describes arriving at Orangeburg at sixteen and feeling he was in heaven, surrounded by hundreds of Black girls he was not related to, unlike Massillon where he seemed kin to every Black girl in town. He thought his father brilliant for sending him to experience his culture.

title: Thanksgiving in Sumter and the Beating
summary: McDew narrates being radicalized over Thanksgiving 1959 in Sumter, South Carolina, where roommate Charles Gatson took him home. As designated driver he was pulled over, struck for not saying yes sir to a white officer, and when he fought back, coming from a tough steel town, two officers beat him bloody, breaking his arm and jaw.
related_events: McDew's Thanksgiving 1959 arrests in Sumter, SC

title: Three Arrests in Two Days
summary: McDew recounts his first arrest for disturbing the peace and disobeying an officer, a second arrest six hours later for refusing the segregated baggage car and taking a seat in the white car of the train, and a third for cutting through Orangeburg's Edisto Gardens park on a day Black people were barred. He quickly learned how much was off-limits under segregation law.
related_events: McDew's Thanksgiving 1959 arrests in Sumter, SC

title: Greensboro Sit-In and Becoming Spokesman
summary: McDew describes how, after the February 1, 1960 sit-in by North Carolina A&T students in Greensboro, South Carolina State students asked him to be their spokesman because he spoke to white people as equals. He initially refused, expecting to leave for Ohio within weeks and never come further south than Columbus for the Michigan-Ohio State game.

title: Leading the Orangeburg Movement and Mass Arrests
summary: McDew recounts becoming head of the Orangeburg Movement for Civic Improvement, leading sit-ins and wade-ins. He was arrested in Columbia delivering a petition to Governor Ernest Fritz Hollings against segregation of public places, and arrested with about 800 others in Orangeburg demanding desegregation.

title: Refusing Lifelong Nonviolence at Shaw
summary: McDew narrates the April 1960 Shaw University conference convened by Dr. King and SCLC. He refused King's proposal that students join SCLC and accept nonviolence as a way of life, arguing that for him nonviolence was strictly a tactic, citing Gandhi to insist that a moral appeal could not work in the midst of an amoral society.

title: Down the Hall: Naming SNCC
summary: McDew describes how those who would not join Dr. King met down the hall at Shaw and formed what became the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee. First considered the student coordinating committee, the group kept the word nonviolent at the urging of Nashville students taught by James Lawson, who deeply believed in its viability.

title: Electing Marion Barry and the Conventions
summary: McDew recalls the summer plan to take a student statement to the Democratic and Republican conventions, seeking a civil rights plank, and the election of Marion Barry as SNCC's first chairman to carry those concerns. That fall SCLC provided money, office space, and material as the new organization set itself up.

title: Becoming Chair and the Name Snick
summary: McDew confirms replacing Marion Barry as formal chair at the fall Atlanta meeting and describes SNCC's developing organizational personality. He recounts how a reporter coined the nickname Snick from the initials SNCC, and how the group's identity was shaped by what it did.

title: Jail-No-Bail at Rock Hill
summary: McDew explains the jail-no-bail program developed as the group asked how long it could continue if it kept getting arrested and bailing out. The tactic was tried in Rock Hill, South Carolina, supported by Rev. C.A. Ivory and state NAACP head I. DeQuincey Newman, who had a strong presence in the state.

title: To McComb for Voter Registration
summary: McDew describes SNCC going to McComb, Mississippi in Southwest Mississippi to register voters once the freedom rides had taken place. By then SNCC had its own identity, separate from CORE, and was hammering out two programs: direct action and voter registration.

title: A Five-Year Lifespan by Design
summary: McDew explains that SNCC planned its own death at its founding, giving itself a finite span of about five years. The number satisfied parents who would not accept their college students simply finding themselves, and matched the group's sense that members would burn out, be jailed, or be killed within that time.

title: Power Sits in the House and Senate
summary: McDew lays out SNCC's analysis of American political structure: real power lay not in the presidency but in the House and Senate, held through committee chairmanships won by longevity. Those seats belonged to white Southern men from Black Belt areas with heavy Black populations where Black people did not vote, out of disinterest and fear.

title: Targeting the Worst Black Belt Counties
summary: McDew names the strategy of going to the worst counties for retaliation: Dougherty, Terrell, and Baker counties in Southwest Georgia, and Amite, Pike, and Walthall counties in Southwest Mississippi. He notes counties 60 to 65 percent Black where no one was registered, the home ground of senators like John Stennis, James Eastland, Robert Byrd, and Herman Talmadge.

title: Disrupting the Balance of Power
summary: McDew explains the simple logic of unseating an entrenched senator like John Stennis: register enough Black voters that even a freshman replacement, however hostile, would arrive with no power and no time to act on threats. Doing this in enough places could change the country in a short period.

title: The Pledge to Pay the Blood Price
summary: McDew describes the central SNCC pledge that the real battle was the vote, that violence was part of the game, and that beatings, bombings, or killings would not turn them away as it had scared off earlier generations. They accepted there would be a blood price to pay, and committed to pay it to change the country.

title: Education and the Posse Foundation
summary: McDew names education as the key to the future and recounts speaking to the Posse Foundation at Colby College in Maine, the first school outside the South in 45 years where his audience was all Black American students, attending top universities. He notes President Obama gave a considerable amount of his Nobel Peace Prize money to the Posse Foundation.
related_events: Obama Nobel Peace Prize donation to the Posse Foundation

title: Correcting the Miseducation of Children
summary: McDew argues for broader education, more Black teachers, and a civil rights course like Mississippi's requirement in every state, noting he had to come South to learn his own role in Black history. He says the nation's soul will be strengthened by understanding the personality of SNCC and the civil rights struggle, which is not over.

title: Democracy, the New Museum, and Closing
summary: McDew reflects that freedom is not free and democracy takes constant struggle, tying the American dream of the immigrants he knew as a child to that work. He welcomes the new Smithsonian museum as important to defining citizenship, gives thanks for waging the struggle, and closes the interview with the interviewer.
related_events: Construction of the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture
